# Round-2 rare-word review: WHITELIST candidates & NO-SAFE-SWAP (owner ruling needed)

*Generated by `scripts/40_split_round2_proposals.py` from the round-2 batch proposal files.* WHITELIST entries carry the agent's compelling reason to protect the word; NO-SAFE-SWAP entries should change but have no common-KJV candidate.

## alert → alert (no change) — Genesis 1:26
*(from batch_0001_proposals_v2.md)*
- verdict: WHITELIST
- suggestion: "watchful" or "wary" as a fallback common-word candidate, should the owner ever reject the whitelist
- whitelist-case: This reading was already the subject of an owner-approved memory restoration (see agent memory: Genesis 1:26 "alert" as one of the memory-testimony readings the project has already ruled on, alongside "rugged," "fearless and daring," and "swift"). Per the Premise Revision, corroborated memory outranks corpus advisory evidence and cannot be undone by a statistical rarity flag alone.
- rationale: Because this exact wording was already settled through the memory-restoration workflow rather than through ordinary witness attestation, it should not be treated as an ordinary rare-word corruption candidate. Recommend whitelisting and flagging to the owner only if new memory evidence contradicts it.

## archangel → archangel (no change) — I Thessalonians 4:16
*(from batch_0001_proposals_v2.md)*
- verdict: WHITELIST
- suggestion: "chief angel" as a fallback common-phrase candidate (word_counts "angel" = 200; matches BBE's "chief angel")
- whitelist-case: "Archangel" names a specific rank — a chief or prince among angels (the figure of Michael) — distinct from an ordinary angel. No common corpus word carries that rank distinction without loss; substituting bare "angel" would flatten a real hierarchical referent the verse is naming.
- rationale: The referent (a named class of superior angelic being) is a specific, non-fungible concept, the same kind of case as round-1's almond/anvil whitelist grounds. Recommend whitelisting; if rejected, "chief angel" is the best fallback.

## archangel → archangel (no change) — Jude 1:9
*(from batch_0001_proposals_v2.md)*
- verdict: WHITELIST
- suggestion: "chief angel" as fallback (as above)
- whitelist-case: Same specific-referent argument as I Thessalonians 4:16 — here naming Michael by his particular rank in a context (disputing with the devil over Moses's body) where that rank is thematically significant.
- rationale: Recommend whitelisting for the same reason as the parallel occurrence; the two should be ruled consistently since they are the same word group.

## awl → awl (no change) — Exodus 21:6
*(from batch_0001_proposals_v2.md)*
- verdict: WHITELIST
- suggestion: "boring instrument" as fallback common-phrase candidate (matches BBE's "sharp-pointed instrument"), should the owner reject whitelisting
- whitelist-case: "Awl" names one specific tool with a specific ritual function (piercing a bondservant's ear at the doorpost as a sign of lifelong service). No common corpus word names this implement without paraphrase, and the ritual specificity of the act (ear + door/doorpost + awl) is part of what the verse is describing.
- rationale: This is the same category of unique-referent case as round-1's almond/anvil whitelist. Recommend whitelisting; "boring instrument" is the best fallback if rejected.

## axles → axles (no change) — I Kings 7:32
*(from batch_0001_proposals_v2.md)*
- verdict: WHITELIST
- suggestion: "axletrees," the word used elsewhere by witnesses for this same part — but note it is entirely unattested (0) in KJV_restored, so it cannot actually be safely substituted; no other common-word candidate was found
- whitelist-case: "Axles" names one specific mechanical part of the bronze laver-stands' wheel-assembly, in a verse that already separately lists "wheels," "naves," "rims," and "spokes" as distinct parts. There is no remaining common word left to name the axle itself without collapsing two distinct described parts into one.
- rationale: This is a technical-description case: the verse's own internal structure (four separately-named wheel components) leaves no common substitute that would not create redundancy with an adjacent term. Recommend whitelisting; flag to the owner that the natural KJV substitute "axletrees" is itself unattested in this corpus and so is not a safe swap either.

## backbitings → evil speakings — II Corinthians 12:20
*(from batch_0001_proposals_v2.md)*
- verdict: WHITELIST
- suggestion: "evil speakings," which is in fact already present in this exact verse's own vice-list in KJV_restored
- whitelist-case: This is a case where the corpus's own text already supplies the intended common-word form ("evil speakings") standing beside "backbitings" in the same list; the two are near-synonyms already glossing each other in context, so the rare word functions as intensifying variety within a deliberately long list of near-synonymous vices (debates, envyings, wraths, strifes...), a rhetorical pattern (a "heaping up" list) that period usage favors.
- rationale: Because "evil speakings" already stands in the same verse, replacing "backbitings" with the identical word would create an odd duplicate ("evil speakings...evil speakings") rather than a fix. Recommend whitelisting this occurrence specifically due to the list-duplication problem; if the owner still wants a change, "whisperings" (a synonym already used by several witnesses for a nearby vice in the same list) could be considered, but that risks its own duplication and is not proposed here as a clean swap.

## battering → battering (no change) — Ezekiel 4:2
*(from batch_0001_proposals_v2.md)*
- verdict: WHITELIST
- suggestion: "engines of war" as fallback common-phrase candidate (matches Geneva1599 "engins of warre"), should the owner reject whitelisting
- whitelist-case: "Battering rams" names one specific siege engine, a real object of the ancient Near Eastern world (attested in Assyrian and Babylonian siege warfare, the plain historical setting of this Ezekiel oracle against Jerusalem). No common corpus word names this device without paraphrase, and the specificity of the engine is part of what the prophecy is describing (a real siege, not a generic attack).
- rationale: Same unique-referent category as round-1's almond/anvil and this batch's awl/axles. Recommend whitelisting; "engines of war" is the best fallback if rejected, though it loses the specific device named.

- rationale: Recommend whitelisting for consistency with Ezekiel 4:2.

## breed → (no safe swap) — Leviticus 19:19
*(from batch_0002_proposals_v2.md)*
- verdict: NO-SAFE-SWAP
- suggestion: best failed candidate is "gender" (0 hits) / "engender" (0 hits), the words most witnesses actually use here; "conceive" (14 hits) is attested but wrongly implies a female-only sense inappropriate to cattle husbandry generally.
- NEW: Ye shall keep my statutes. Thou shalt not let thy cattle breed with a diverse kind: thou shalt not sow thy field with mingled seed: neither shall a garment mingled of linen and woollen come upon thee.
- rationale: no common corpus word (≥5 hits) precisely covers "cause livestock of different kinds to mate" without either being absent (gender/engender) or misapplied (conceive implies female gestation, not general mating); leaving "breed" stands pending a better-attested option.

## breedeth → (no safe swap) — Job 21:10
*(from batch_0002_proposals_v2.md)*
- verdict: NO-SAFE-SWAP
- suggestion: best failed candidate is "gendereth" (0 hits); "bringeth forth" is attested but is already used in the same verse for the cow's calving, so re-using it for the ox would erase the deliberate distinction between siring and calving.
- NEW: Their ox breedeth, and faileth not; their cow bringeth forth, and casteth not her calf.
- rationale: the verse pairs two distinct verbs (ox begets / cow calves); the only common substitute available ("bringeth forth") is already spoken for by the second clause, so swapping it in for "breedeth" would flatten a meaningful parallel rather than preserve it.

## citron → (whitelist) — Revelation 18:12
*(from batch_0002_proposals_v2.md)*
- verdict: WHITELIST
- suggestion: (best-effort alternative if forced to swap) "sweet wood" (using "sweet", count 109) — but this loses the specific referent, a named aromatic timber (citron/thyine wood, prized in Roman luxury trade) distinct from ordinary "sweet" or "precious" wood already listed in the same verse.
- NEW: The merchandise of gold, and silver, and precious stones, and of pearls, and fine linen, and purple, and silk, and scarlet, and all sweet wood, and all manner vessels of ivory, and all manner vessels of most precious wood, and of brass, and iron, and marble,
- whitelist-case: this verse is a list of specific luxury-trade materials (gold, silver, ivory, marble, etc.), and "citron wood" (like Wycliffe/DRC's "thyine wood") names one particular aromatic timber species from that trade — a real and specific referent, not a generic descriptor. The corpus's only common alternative, "sweet," is already applied to a different item in the same list ("sweet... smelling," Song of Solomon usage) and would flatten a specific named commodity into a vague adjective, losing exactly the kind of concrete detail this catalogue of Roman luxury goods depends on.
- rationale: given the choice between inventing a vague "sweet wood" and keeping the specific named commodity, whitelisting preserves the verse's character as an itemized list of real trade goods; the corpus offers no attested word (≥5 hits) that names this particular timber without loss.

## cricket → (no safe swap) — Leviticus 11:22
*(from batch_0003_proposals_v2.md)*
- verdict: NO-SAFE-SWAP
- suggestion: best failed candidate is "beetle" (0 hits, though used by Webster/RWebster/YLT/UKJV) — no corpus-attested word names this fourth distinct insect kind.
- NEW: (no change proposed — CUR retained) Even these of them ye may eat; the locust after his kind, and the bald locust after his kind, and the cricket after his kind, and the grasshopper after his kind.
- rationale: The verse already uses "locust" and "grasshopper" for two of its four insect kinds, so those words cannot be reused (rule 12 duplicate concern) as replacements for the third; "beetle" and "palmerworm" (3 hits) are the only witnessed alternatives and both remain below the reliability threshold. No common-corpus word names a fourth distinct insect kind without collision or excessive rarity, so this is left unswapped for owner review.

The "speaking council" vs. "school" divergence is a separate, unanimous-witness anomaly that should be logged for owner review rather than silently swapped here, since fixing it would require introducing an otherwise-unattested word.

## wantonness → self-will / own will — Genesis 49:6
*(from batch_0009_proposals_v2.md)*
- verdict: NO-SAFE-SWAP
- suggestion: "self-will" (0 hits in KJV_restored corpus — fails the ≥5 attestation bar) is the best-attested alternative in the witnesses (Geneva1599, Tyndale, Webster, RWebster all read "selfe will"/"self-will"); no other common KJV_restored word (checked "pleasure" 66, "lust" 19, "own" 594) carries the specific sense of unrestrained willfulness/wantonness without drifting into unrelated senses (bodily pleasure, illicit desire, mere possession).
- NEW: O my soul, come not thou into their secret; unto their assembly, mine honour, be not thou joined: for in their anger they slew a man, and in their self-will they digged down a wall.
- rationale: "Wantonness" is a hapax here; every witness converges on "self-will" as the referent (willful destructiveness), but that exact compound is itself unattested elsewhere in the corpus, so no swap clears the ≥5 bar cleanly. Given no common single word captures this sense without loss, I recommend NO-SAFE-SWAP and flag "self-will" as the best candidate should the bar be relaxed for compounds attested near-unanimously across witnesses.

## witch → sorceress — Isaiah 57:3
*(from batch_0009_proposals_v2.md)*
- verdict: NO-SAFE-SWAP
- suggestion: "sorceress" (only 1 hit in KJV_restored — fails the ≥5 bar) is the best failed candidate: it is the near-unanimous witness reading (Geneva1599, DRC, Webster, RWebster, Darby, ASV, ACV, BSB all read "sorceress"/"witches"), but is itself rare in this corpus. The male-gendered "sorcerer" (5 hits, common) is attested enough but wrongly changes the sex of the referent, which the parallel "seed of the adulterer and the whore" (both apparently feminine imagery) makes significant.
- NEW: But draw near hither, ye sons of the sorceress, the seed of the adulterer and the whore.
- rationale: No common (≥5) corpus word names a female practitioner of divination distinct from a male "sorcerer" or plural "sorcerers." Using the male form would erase a referent distinction the verse's parallelism appears to depend on, so I recommend NO-SAFE-SWAP over a gender-incorrect common swap, while naming "sorceress" as the best (if under-attested) fallback.

## woodsman → hewer — Isaiah 14:8
*(from batch_0009_proposals_v2.md)*
- verdict: NO-SAFE-SWAP
- suggestion: "hewer" (0 hits, unattested) is the near-unanimous witness reading (Geneva1599, YLT, ASV, BBE-adjacent all read "hewer"), but fails the ≥5 bar entirely; no other common corpus word (checked "feller" 0) names specifically "one who cuts down trees" without broadening to a generic laborer.
- NEW: Yea, the fir trees rejoice at thee, and the cedars of Lebanon, saying, Since thou art laid down, no hewer is come up against us.
- rationale: Both candidate trade-nouns for "tree-feller" ("hewer," "feller") are wholly unattested in this corpus, so no swap clears the attestation bar. I recommend NO-SAFE-SWAP and name "hewer" as the best (near-unanimous witness) fallback rather than force in an unrelated common word that would lose the specific referent (one who cuts timber).
